Credit Risk · Model Validation · Tier A

The portfolio under-reserves by $10.0bn — and every standard risk report says it is healthy

A calibration review of a 50,000-loan, $165bn credit portfolio: what the booked expected loss misses, why the stress test understates it, and what provision should replace it.

Prepared for the Chief Risk Officer and Head of Model Validation  ·  22 July 2026  ·  Data 2015-01 to 2024-12  ·  Synthetic dataset — no figure describes a real institution

SituationThe portfolio runs a complete Basel / IFRS 9 apparatus — PD, LGD, EAD, EL and RWA on every loan, a ten-year rating-migration history, vintage curves and a six-scenario stress suite. Every number a risk committee asks for exists.
ComplicationIt books $1.92bn of expected loss against $11.91bn actually lost. Its stress test reports losses falling under a zero shock. Three of its tables give three different default counts for the same book. None of this is visible in the reporting the committee actually reviews.
ResolutionBoth loss defects distort the level of risk while leaving the ranking intact — and standard risk reporting measures only ranking.
The portfolio under-reserves by $10.0bn because expected loss is booked on a 1-year PD that is also mis-calibrated at investment grade. Because both defects move the level and not the rank, every chart in the MI pack looks healthy.
  1. The shortfall is two defects of near-equal size. A 1-year-vs-lifetime horizon mismatch accounts for 51% ($5.14bn) and genuine mis-calibration for 49% ($4.85bn), of which $2.64bn sits in investment grade. LGD is sound and needs no change.
  2. The published stress test is arithmetically broken. Its zero-shock baseline shows expected loss falling 10.2%; correcting the inconsistent EL base restates every scenario upward by 18.4pp.
  3. The apparent post-2020 improvement in credit quality is an artifact. The trend reverses sign depending on the sample (ρ -0.52 all cohorts vs +0.95 observed-only), because one calendar shock — not seasoning — drives every vintage curve.
  4. Fix the parameter, not the model. The provision should be $12.46bn. But the incumbent PD posts the best AUC of any model tested (0.883 vs 0.864 for XGBoost), so a rebuild would spend effort on the one dimension that is not broken.
Recommendation Model Validation should recalibrate PD at grade level on realized experience — raising the provision from $1.92bn to $12.46bn (95% CI $11.85–13.11bn) — before the next provisioning run. The horizon correction alone lifts it to $7.39bn and can be applied immediately, since it is a definitional change requiring no new model. Risk Reporting should restate all 60 rows of the stress table on a consistent EL base in the same cycle. Both teams should adopt calibration-drift monitoring and the three-window default reconciliation as standing pre-close controls. Do not deploy the retrained model — no protected-subgroup fairness audit is possible on this data.
16%
of realized loss covered by booked EL
64×
AAA default understated vs booked PD
$12.5bn
recommended provision (from $1.9bn)
0.88
incumbent AUC on holdout — ranking was never the problem

Part I · Diagnostic

Key Line 1 — The shortfall is two defects, not one

The obvious reading of a $10.0bn gap is “the PD model is broken”. That is only half right, and the half it misses would leave roughly $5bn unaddressed. Expected loss is booked as ead × pd_annual × lgd — a one-year figure — while the loss it is compared against accumulates over each loan’s full ~4.5-year term. Separating the two requires re-booking EL on the lifetime PD and seeing how much of the gap closes on its own.

Part I · Diagnostic

Why it went unnoticed for ten years

A scoring model has two independent properties, and conflating them is what allowed this to persist. Discrimination asks whether the model ranks risk correctly. Calibration asks whether the numbers it emits are right. A model can be strong on the first and useless on the second — and virtually every chart in a standard credit MI pack measures only the first.

Part II · Evidence base

Key Line 2 — The stress test is arithmetically broken

A stress suite is the primary input to a capital plan, so its arithmetic deserves a control. The cheapest available one is already in the file and had not been looked at: a zero-shock baseline, which by construction must produce no change in expected loss.

The two defects compound. Under the severe scenario the bank would report $3.80bn of stressed expected loss. On a corrected arithmetic base and a recalibrated PD the figure is $27.39bn — 7.2× higher. Neither error alone produces that: the recalibration raises the base and the arithmetic correction raises the multiplier applied on top of it.

Part III · Dynamics

Key Line 3 — The apparent quality improvement is an artifact

Vintage analysis is the standard tool for asking “is our underwriting getting better?” On this book it returns a confident answer that does not survive inspection — for two independent reasons that happen to point the same way.

The honest answer to “is credit quality improving?” is that this data cannot say. Reporting either sign as a finding would be a choice of narrative rather than a conclusion from evidence.

Part IV · Decision

Key Line 4 — Fix the parameter, not the model

The natural response to a mis-calibrated parameter is to rebuild it with a better model. That was tested and it is the wrong move here. Six models were run against three baselines on an origination-time holdout — and the incumbent pd_annual posted the highest AUC of anything tested (0.883), with a rating-only logistic within 0.003 of it. Discrimination on this book is saturated.

Appendix

Methodology, limitations and what could not be tested

A1 · Model bake-off (origination-time holdout, 2022Q1–2023Q4)

ModelROC-AUCAvg precisionBrier
0a Never-default (naive)0.50000.07810.0781
0b Rating-only logistic0.88010.33910.0643
0c Booked pd_annual (INCUMBENT)0.88260.38370.0670
1 Logistic regression0.83470.35400.3112
2 Random forest0.87320.44040.1758
3 XGBoost0.86400.42710.0637

Train = 2015Q1–2021Q4 originations (n = 38,923, base rate 15.6%); test = 2022Q1–2023Q4 (n = 11,077, base rate 7.8%). The base-rate halving is the 2020Q2 shock composition, not sampling noise. 13 columns were banned as features, including pd_annual itself, which appears only as the benchmark.

A2 · The three-window default reconciliation

Three tables report three default counts for the same 50,000 loans. They are not inconsistent — they answer over three different observation windows, and reconcile to the unit:

SourceDefaultsReconciling adjustment
loan_portfolio (full simulated lifetime)6,950 default dates run to 2033-09-01
vintage_analysis at month 606,531 less 419 defaults after month 60
portfolio_metrics (calendar)6,288 less 662 defaults dated after 2024-12

662 of the 6,950 defaults have not yet occurred as of the data cut. Every model was therefore run against two targets — full-lifetime and as-of-2024-12 — with the divergence reported rather than resolved silently.

A3 · Statistical methods

Binomial exact tests per grade with Wilson intervals; Benjamini–Hochberg FDR across the grade family; Kruskal–Wallis with ε² and Holm-corrected Mann–Whitney for LGD by collateral; Spearman ρ for monotone trend; χ² with Cramér’s V for migration; Kaplan–Meier, log-rank and Cox proportional hazards via statsmodels.duration; a Binomial/logit GLM for recovery rate. At n = 50,000 every p-value is astronomically small, so effect sizes lead every claim and p-values appear only in tables.

An assumption that fails, stated rather than buried. The binomial tests treat defaults as independent within a grade. They are not — Exhibit 6 shows every cohort defaulting together in 2020Q2, i.e. a common macro factor. Correlated events widen the true confidence intervals beyond the binomial ones shown, which makes the test conservative for large misses and unreliable for small ones. Every verdict in this report keys off effect size, not significance.

A4 · Robustness and corroboration

Horizon convention. The calibration finding was pre-committed to be withdrawn if it depended on how an annual PD is converted to a lifetime one. Compound (1−(1−pd)t) and linear (pd×t) agree within 0.7% across AAA–BBB, where the finding lives. They diverge 27.8% at CCC, which is expected arithmetic — the linear form over-counts at high PD — and CCC is the one grade with no finding to defend.

Independent cross-check. A Markov estimate built from the rating-migration table alone, never touching pd_annual, reproduces the shape of the defect: widest at investment grade, closing to nothing at CCC. It matches realized default at CCC (1.00×) and B (1.05×) but remains 3.4–8.8× short at AAA–BBB. Because the two tables describe different populations with no join key between them, it is used as a directional cross-check and a lower bound — not as the recalibration target.

A5 · Fairness audit

AuditResult
Protected demographic subgroups IMPOSSIBLE — no age, gender, geography, ownership or firm-size attribute exists in any of the five tables
Sector (10 groups) PASS — AUC spread 0.061; calibration uniformly off, not sector-specific
Credit-score quintile FAIL — calibration degrades monotonically 0.72× → 0.12× from lowest to highest quality; AUC falls to 0.62 in the top quintile

The retrained model would systematically over-price credit for the highest-quality borrowers — over-predicting their default rate 8.4×. Note the symmetry: the incumbent fails at investment grade and the retrained model fails at the top of the score distribution. Both fail at the safe end, for different reasons. Combined with the impossible demographic audit, this is why the recommendation is recalibrate the parameter, do not deploy the model.

A6 · Limitations

  • The data is synthetic. No figure here describes a real institution. The transferable artifacts are the methods — the calibration test, the reconciliation harness, the zero-shock control and the two-way trend test.
  • The book contains future defaults. 662 defaults are dated after the 2024-12 cut, out to 2033-09-01; the lifetime target is a simulation outcome, not an observation.
  • No join key exists between the five tables, so no multi-level (loan within issuer within macro) model is possible and the Markov cross-check cannot be reconciled to the loan book.
  • No borrower financials, collections timeline or IFRS 9 staging. The restated provision is a parameter correction, not a full ECL calculation.
  • Sector, loan type and collateral carry no PD signal by construction (all span <2pp of default rate), so no segment-level recommendation appears anywhere in this report.
  • The monitoring plan is unvalidated. This is a static historical file with no live scoring, so no drift threshold in it has been tested.

A7 · Reproducibility

Six notebooks, 01_ingestion → 06_reporting, run restart-and-run-all clean. random_state = 42 throughout. 28 schema rules asserted at ingestion. Every number in this report is read from reports/_key_figures.json, written by the analysis notebooks — none is hardcoded here.

Credit risk calibration review · Tier A · generated 2026-07-22 from _key_figures.json · synthetic data, not a real portfolio
